items.text: Your edge over professionals isn't intelligence — it's structure. A fund manager reports quarterly, faces client redemptions, and risks their career holding an unloved position through a 40% drawdown, even when they're right. So they hug benchmarks. You have no clients, no benchmark, no quarterly review. You can hold a sound thesis through three ugly years — an action that would get a professional fired. That freedom IS the edge.

Amazon 1999–2001: the stock fell 90% while the business grew MORE valuable — warehouses built, customers added, unit economics improving. Price and intrinsic value moved in opposite directions for two years. Lesson: price can be a completely unreliable signal of what's happening to business value, for long stretches, in either direction. Long-horizon investors price the business, not the stock.

CAGR = (End/Start)^(1/years) − 1. It's the smoothed annual rate that turns the start value into the end value. ₹1 at 15% for 20 years = ₹16. At 25%, ₹86. The gap between good and great compounding is enormous over long horizons — which is why durability of returns matters more than one spectacular year.
Why can't most fund managers simply choose a 10-year view, even when they agree it works?
options: Regulations forbid holding stocks that long
Quarterly reporting + client redemptions make interim underperformance career-fatal
Long-term data isn't available to them
items.explanation: It's structural, not intellectual: clients redeem during drawdowns, so managers who lag for two years lose assets and jobs — even if ultimately right.

items.text: During 1999–2001 Amazon's stock fell 90%. What was its intrinsic value most plausibly doing?
options: Falling roughly in line with the stock
Rising — infrastructure, customers and unit economics were improving
Flat — nothing changes in two years
items.explanation: The business compounded value while sentiment collapsed. Price and value can diverge for years — the long-horizon investor's opportunity.

items.text: A stock goes from ₹200 to ₹520 in 10 years. Roughly what CAGR is that?
items.explanation: CAGR = (520/200)^(0.1) − 1 = 2.6^0.1 − 1 ≈ 10%. Dividing total return by years (option A) ignores compounding and overstates the rate.
